---
title: My dynamic report
output: html_fragment
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, error=FALSE)
```

```{r load_data}
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Load your dataset
latest_data <- read.csv("latest_data.csv")

# Rename columns to simpler names
latest_data <- latest_data %>%
  rename(
    timestamp = Timestamp,
    time_seen = At.what.time.did.you.see.this.ad...please.use.24h.time.,
    ad_type = What.type.of.Instagram.ad.was.this.,
    product_type = What.type.of.product.or.service.was.being.advertised.,
    seconds_viewed = Approximately.how.many.seconds.did.you.spend.looking.at.this.ad.,
    relevance = How.relevant.did.this.ad.feel.to.you.
  )

# Create summary values
avg_seconds <- mean(latest_data$seconds_viewed)
avg_relevance <- mean(latest_data$relevance)
```

## Instagram Ad Engagement Analysis

This dynamic report summarises the Instagram ads I observed over several days. 
It includes summary values and visualisations that automatically update whenever 
new data is added to my Google Form.

On average, I spent **`r avg_seconds` seconds** looking at each ad.

The average relevance score was **`r avg_relevance`** out of 5.

```{r}
ggplot(latest_data, aes(x = ad_type)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "skyblue") +
  labs(title = "Types of Instagram Ads",
       x = "Ad Type",
       y = "Count") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

```{r}
ggplot(latest_data, aes(x = product_type)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "orange") +
  labs(title = "Types of Products Advertised",
       x = "Product Type",
       y = "Count") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```
